text:
TheParis Olympicshave proven to be enthralling, with Team GB winning their 13th gold medal on Thursday afternoon asEllie Aldridge stormed to victoryin the women’s kitesurfing final. Her triumph came after she had dominated her rivals at the Marseille Marina.

Team GB began Friday in fifth place in the overall medal table after also claiming 17 silvers and 21 bronzes.

Team USA led the way ahead of the final few days of competition after winning 30 gold medals, with China in second place with 29. Australia had 18 golds to their name, while hosts France had 14.

USA star Noah Lyles was the name on everyone’s lips on Thursday evening after he wasdiagnosed with Covidjust moments after he had missed out on a gold medal in the 200m.

The 27-year-old had been the overwhelming favourite to win the competition but finished behind winner Letsile Tebogo and second-place Kenny Bednarek.

And former 400m record holder Michael Johnson then exclaimed: “HavingCovidand still being out here in close proximity with other people - I know there’s probably not a policy at these Games for that but morally I’m not sure about it.”

Express Sport will bring you all the latest Olympics news throughout the day. So follow our live blog below for all the updates.

That's all we've got time for tonight.

Thanks for joining us to follow the latest news from the Olympics on another jam-packed day of sporting action.

We'll be back tomorrow to guide you through the penultimate day of the Games.

Catch you then!

Imane Khelif has secured Olympic glory with a dominant victory over Yang Liu of China in the women's 66kg gold medal match.

The Algerian fighter, who has been at the centre of a gender row throughout the Games, won every round on the judges' scorecards.

The two fighters shared a sporting embrace at the final bell, with Khelif celebrating with her coaches and Yang trudging back to her corner knowing she had not done enough.

Khelif broke into a victory dance after the decision was read out as she revelled in her well-deserved Olympic triumph.

Buse Naz Cakiroglu was forced to restrain her own coachafter he reacted furiously at the end of her gold medal fight against Wu Yu of China.

The pair had been battling for top spot on the podium in the women's 50kg classification, with Yu cruising to a routine victory.

Cakiroglu's coach was left enraged by the decision and needed to be restrained by the fighter and his fellow coaches.



Thierry Henry was left heartbroken as his France side were beaten 5-3 by Spain in the men's football final.

The hosts mounted a late comeback to force a 30-minute period of extra time, with Jean-Phillipe Mateta scoring a penalty in stoppage time to level the scores.

However, a brace from Sergio Camello saw Spain come up trumps and secure the gold medal without needing a penalty shoot-out.

Thomas Bach, president of the International Olympic Committee, is adamant that Imane Khelif should be allowed to compete at the Games.

"This is not a question of inclusion, this has never played a role in all this, this is a question of justice, women must be allowed to take part in women’s competitions," he said.

"It is not as easy [as those] in this cultural war may want to portray it, that the XX or XY is the clear distinction between men and women.

"This is scientifically not true anymore. Therefore, they have the right to participate in the women’s competition. This has nothing to do with inclusion in any way."

Khelif will face Yang Liu in the women's 66kg gold medal match later this evening.

Katarina Johnson-Thompson has been forced to settle for silver in the women's heptathlon in spite of her best efforts.

She fell just short of overhauling German rival Nafi Thiam despite a personal-best run in the 800 metres.

Just 36 points separate the pair in the end, with Johnson-Thompson claiming her first Olympic medal as a well-deserved consolation.



Team GB have added to their growing medal tally with bronze on the athletics track and in the cycling velodrome.

A third-placed finish in the men's 4x100m relay final was followed up by another bronze for Jack Carlin in the men's individual track cycling sprint.

Carlin was lucky to avoid being disqualified after crashing into Dutch rival Jeffrey Hoogland while they were battling for position.

The race was eventually rerun after a lengthy stoppage, with Carlin pulling off a late surge to keep Hoogland at arm's length.

Team GB have won another silver medal in the women's 4x100m relay, with the United States claiming gold.

Dina Asher-Smith made a storming start on the first bend with Imani-Lara Lansiquot doing well on the back straight.

Daryll Neita put plenty of pressure on Sha'Carri Richardson on the home straight but the American quartet have taken the top prize.

A couple of dodgy changeovers ultimately wrecked Team GB's hopes of beating the USA at the line.

Team GB have taken the silver medal in the women's madison track cycling event.

Elinor Barker and Neah Evans left it late to beat the Netherlands to second place, behind only gold medallists Italy.

It takes Britain's medal tally up to 53 with 14 golds, 81 silvers and 21 bronze medals.

Letsile Tebogo offered a blunt assessement of sprint rival Noah Lyles after beating him in the men's 200 metre final.

Asked if he was hoping to surpass Lyles as the current face of track and field, Tebogo said: "For me, I can’t be the face of athletics because I’m not an arrogant or loud person like Noah.

"So I believe Noah’s the face of athletics."



Liechtenstein’s only athlete at the Olympics has revealed that he was tracked down byAndy Murrayon the first day of the Games.

Mountain biker Romano Puntener said he was approached by Murray, who asked him for a pin badge to boost his collection.

"I was really surprised,"he said. "It was after dinner when me and my mechanic took some bikes and rode through the village and he saw me.

"I didn’t expect to meet him on my first evening there at the Olympic Village. I was a bit surprised that he asked me for a Liechtenstein pin.

"I knew that pin trading is a big thing at the Olympics but I didn’t know that superstars like Andy Murray do that.”

Olympics star Nyjah Huston wants higher quality Olympic medals after his bronze prize showed signs of rust just a week after he had clinched third place in the skateboarding.

The 29-year-old posted photos and videos of his medal to hisInstagramstory as he wrote: “Alright, so these Olympic medals look great when they're brand new, but after letting it sit on my skin with some sweat and letting my friends wear it over the weekend, they're apparently not as high quality as you would think.

“Look at that thing. It's looking rough, even the front. It's starting to chip off a little. I don't know, Olympic medals, maybe you gotta step up the quality a little bit.” He added that his medal looked like it “went to war and back”.

BBCviewers watching the 2024 Olympics have flooded social media with complaints following a mistake by commentator Ed Leigh after Toby Roberts had won an incredible gold medal in the men's combined climbing.

Leigh caused uproar by mistakenly calling the national anthem 'God Save the Queen' after the podium ceremony on Friday.

Roberts clinched Team GB's 14th gold in Paris by accumulating a score of 155.2, narrowly beating Japan's Anraku Sorato - the event favourite.

And as Roberts stood proudly between Sorata and Austria's Jakob Schubert with the national anthem playing, Leigh said: "Very stirring rendition of God Save the Queen. And in just his first full season of elite-level climbing, Toby Roberts has left his mark."

Keely Hodgkinson has revealed that she enjoyed an alcohol-laden party after winning her Olympic gold medal in the women’s 800m final. The young Brit was working on just two hours of sleep the following day.

Offering fans an insight into the moments that followed her historic triumph, Hodgkinson told BBC Sport: “So the first thing I did was actually anti-doping, so I went and weed in front of a random person.

“After that, I checked my phone, did a bit of a cooldown. I went to the press conference… it’s late at night at this point, it’s about 11.30pm or 12, [so] you are getting quite tired. Then after that, I was fortunate enough to go and see my family and friends in a really cool bar in Paris.

“And there was over 40 people here supporting me that I know so that was really great, and I was there until about two or three in the morning. They were chanting, screaming, throwing drinks, having the most insane time.”



Team GB are up to 14 golds at the Paris Olympics after Toby Roberts reigned supreme in the men's combined climbing. Roberts was alongside Hamish McArthur as Great Britain's first ever male climbers at an Olympics.

The 19-year-old from Surrey's score of 155.2 saw him claim gold at his maiden Games after Japan's Anraku Sorato - the final climber - fell in the lead discipline just before the 60-point markers.

Team GB star Max Burgin apologised during an interview with the BBC moments after qualifying for the Olympics 800m final on Friday. The 22-year-old cemented his place in the showpiece event after crossing the line in third place with a time of 1:43.50m.

“I couldn’t be happier,” Burgin explained before momentarily finding himself lost for words. “I don’t know what to say. It’s sort of come out of nowhere. My fastest time this year has been 1:45m. I knew I could run faster, but to get through in a PB – and I felt quite good doing it as well - so yeah.

“I wouldn’t have believed it a month ago. I wouldn’t have believed it a few weeks ago to be honest. I really don’t know what to say, sorry.”



Matthew Hudson-Smith was forced to flee from an interview with the BBC to vomit after Team GB secured their spot in the men's 4x400m relay final on Friday morning.

Hudson-Smith joined Samuel Reardon, Toby Harries and Charlie Dobson in a strong quartet that finished second in their heat at the Stade de France.

Katarina Johnson-Thompson had a short and sweet message for BBC pundit Denise Lewis after a flying start in the women's heptathlon. The Team GB athlete took the lead in the event on Thursday as she bids for a first-ever Olympic medal.

The 31-year-old impressed with a personal best of 14.44m in the shot put, before going on to record a time of 23.44 seconds in the 200m sprint. That put Johnson-Thompson in the lead with 4,055 points.

And in a short but sweet 11-word tribute, Johnson-Thompson said: "That was entirely for Denise Lewis sitting in the BBC studio." She added: "Probably the best thing I've ever done in my career - and I've jumped 1.98m [for high jump]. That topped everything. A little 14m shot put."

Botswana president Mokgweetsi Masisi has declared a public holiday today in honour of Paris Olympics 200m champion Letsile Tebogo, according to Zimbabwean publicationPindula.

The Botswana sprinter stormed to victory ahead of Team USA duo Kenny Bednarek and Noah Lyles at the Stade de France in a time of 19.46s.

Team USA star Kenny Bednarek was asked for his reaction to team-mate Noah Lyles’s decision to run the 200m final despite testing positive for Covid. Lyles hugged silver-medallist Bednarek after clinching bronze.

“I don’t know, I don’t care,” Bednarek explained. “If I get sick, I’ll be fine, if he gets sick, it’s a cold. I don’t view those things as a big deal. I am healthy. I do everything to make sure my body is healthy.

“I don’t get sick easily. I give him a hug regardless of if he’s sick or not. I don’t care about those things.”

The men’s hockey gold medal match between the Netherlands and Germany ended in chaos after a brawl broke out following the conclusion of the showdown.

The Netherlands clinched gold following a dramatic shootout after the clash had ended 1-1. Duco Telgenkamp scored the winning penalty shot but then ran up to Germany goalkeeper Jean-Paul Danneberg and shushed his opponent before slapping his faceguard.

Germany star Niklas Wellen rushed over to confront Telgenkamp, with the Dutchman then putting his hand on his rival’s throat as the scene turned ugly.



We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Team GB star Jade Jones, who won Olympic gold medals in 2012 and 2016, insisted she is not a drugs cheat after suffering a shock first round taekwondo defeat to North Macedonia's Miljana Reljikj.

Jones avoided punishment by the UK Anti-Doping Agency (UKAD) for a doping violation at the end of last year after failing to provide a urine sample.

“I can confirm that I’ve never taken drugs. I’ve done hundreds of tests and since then I’ve done 13 more tests - more than ever. I’m obviously not on drugs, I just lost,” Jones said as she spoke publicly for the first time about the incident.

“I’m very grateful to people for looking into it properly. I made a mistake. They came on dehydration day, I wasn’t in the right mind. You're losing weight and you haven't eaten or drank for a few days. I needed to dehydrate. I become stressed. I thought you could miss three. I signed a piece of paper but I didn’t know what I was signing.

“I was lucky they looked into it and it all got sorted and it got proven I was innocent. I am just so grateful, blessed that they looked into it, that I was cleared, I was in no fault. It was a big story. It wasn’t one thing. I was grateful to be here.”

Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of the Paris Olympics. There are just three days of action remaining in the French capital, with the closing ceremony set to take place on Sunday. So stick around throughout the day for all the updates.

title: Olympics news: IOC chief shares strong Imane Khelif view as Thierry Henry heartbroken | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Tom Parsons, Archie Griggs
description: Express Sport brings you all the latest Paris Olympics news ahead of Sunday's closing ceremony.
